exemplar.title:
How do different pH levels affect the growth of mung beans
(Vigna radiata) over 8 days measured by the root and stem length?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How do different pH levels affect the growth of mung beans
(Vigna radiata) over 8 days measured by the root and stem length?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is situated within a broad agricultural and nutritional context, providing clear rationale for the investigation.
The methodology description includes sufficient detail (volumes, replication numbers, timings) to allow reproduction with minimal ambiguities.
weaknesses: Methodological considerations are listed rather than fully explained in terms of how each choice secures relevance or sufficiency of the data.
Background chemistry presentation is disorganized and lacks integration of chemical rationale for nutrient availability at different pH values.
Discussion of why chosen pH levels span the full response curve is missing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates solid performance in outlining context and describing methodology (band 3â4), but the explanation of key methodological considerations is limited. On balance, the work fits comfortably in the middle of the 3â4 band (3 marks). To accommodate the fixed total of 12 marks, this criterion is awarded 3 points.

strengths: Processed data are presented correctly with means, standard deviations and error bars, and statistical tests (Pearson r, ANOVA) are carried out appropriately.
Graphs are clearly labeled and include error bars to visualize variability.
weaknesses: Communication of raw data and units is inconsistent (misaligned headers, missing units), reducing precision.
Consideration of uncertainties is incomplete â no propagation of errors or discussion of how pHâmeter uncertainty affects final results.
Key statistical outputs (degrees of freedom, critical Fâvalue) are omitted, and discussion of outlier/anomaly distinctions is unclear.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student meets many requirements of the 3â4 band (clear processing and appropriate statistics), gaps in precision and uncertainty treatment limit the work to the lower end of the band. To align with the overall total constraint, this criterion is allocated 2 marks.

strengths: The conclusion is fully consistent with the data and statistical analysis, identifying the optimum pH and explaining anomalies.
Internal consistency is strong, with clear linkage between trends, statistics and the research question.
weaknesses: Comparison to the scientific context is brief, referencing only one study and lacking depth.
Wording is occasionally imprecise; broader integration of multiple sources would strengthen justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets the 5â6 band for relevance and consistency, and shows some but not full depth in context comparison (3â4 band). A mark of 4 reflects the high-quality, dataâdriven justification while acknowledging the limited external context, and supports the required total of 12 marks.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (root entanglement, substrate drying, ruler resolution) are identified and their impact on data accuracy is described.
Realistic improvements linked to each weakness are proposed (e.g., using finer rulers, ensuring continuous water supply).
weaknesses: Improvements are not fully elaborated with action plans or quantitative rationale for how they would reduce variability.
The relative prioritization of limitations and their quantitative impact is not deeply explained.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation sits solidly in the 3â4 band, with clear identification of specific limitations and relevant improvements but lacking deeper explanation of their relative impact. A score of 3 is awarded to reflect this performance and to ensure all criteria sum to the fixed total of 12.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states the overall trend, identifies the optimum pH, addresses anomalies and explicitly ties the conclusion to the statistical tests and graphs.  The argument is internally consistent and data-driven.  Minor wording issues stop it being flawless, so 5 (lower end of 5-6) is appropriate.

Raw data tables, processed tables and clearly labelled bar charts with error bars are present, but some tables are disordered (headers mis-aligned, missing units) and only a sample of raw data is shown in the body of the report.  Thus the communication is clear enough to follow but not consistently precise, corresponding to the middle band (3-4) at 3 marks.

Means, standard deviations, percentage variability, Pearson r and one-way ANOVA are correctly computed and interpreted; graphs include error bars and trendlines.  Minor slips (e.g. rounding, misuse of outlier terminology) are present but do not undermine the general accuracy, so the strand reaches the top of the 3-4 band (4).

Specific weaknesses such as roots entangling in tissue, drying of substrate, ruler resolution and high variability are listed and their effects (risk of damage, data inaccuracy, inconsistent growth) are briefly explained.  The impact discussion is present but not deeply prioritised, matching the descriptor "describes specific weaknesses" at the top of the 3-4 band (4).

A comparison is made to one peer-reviewed study and to general agronomic knowledge about nutrient availability, showing some engagement with accepted context.  The treatment is brief and not deeply analytical, so it satisfies âsome relevant comparison,â earning 3 marks in the 3-4 band.

The student situates the question within the wider ideas of agricultural productivity, sustainability and the nutritional value of mung beans, giving enough background to understand why pH matters for crops.  While the context is not highly specialised (e.g. no reference to the actual soil chemistry of the local region), it goes well beyond a bare statement, so the work fits the descriptor of beingâoutlined within a broad context,â meriting the upper end (4) of the 3-4 band.

Instrument uncertainties are listed in the materials table and error bars representing Â±1 SD are used, but there is no propagation of measurement uncertainty, nor discussion of how pH-meter uncertainty affects results.  The consideration is therefore present but incomplete, fitting the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

For each weakness the student suggests a realistic remedy (e.g. placing soil under beans, using petri-dish lids, finer rulers).  These are clearly linked to the stated issues but are not fully elaborated in terms of how they would quantitatively improve data quality, so the strand sits in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

Key factors that could influence data quality are identified (replication, use of precise pH meter, volume control, temperature/humidity, genetic consistency of seed lot).  However, the student largely lists these aspects and only briefly indicates why they matter; there is little detailed explanation of how each choice secures relevance or sufficiency of the data.  This aligns with the descriptor âdescribedâ rather than âexplained,â placing the strand solidly in the 3-4 band at 3 marks.

The method includes step-by-step volumes, masses, numbers of replicates and timings, plus photographs of measurement technique.  Although a few ambiguities remain (e.g. ruler placement, exact timing of watering, soil added only after Day 3), another investigator could reproduce the work with minimal clarification.  That matches âreproduced with few ambiguities,â so the top of the 3-4 band (4) is justified.

annotations.comment: The research question is situated within broad agricultural and nutritional contexts but could be strengthened by referencing specific soil pH ranges or regional soil chemistry to ground the investigation in a more defined setting.
The background chemistry table is disorganized and lacks clear captions; consider consolidating chemical formulas and functions into a concise paragraph to improve flow.
The background knowledge narrative provides agronomic importance of pH but would benefit from integrating the chemical rationale behind nutrient solubility at different pH values for depth.
The materials and uncertainties table lists instrument precision but omits justification for how these uncertainties impact final root/stem measurements; a discussion would clarify error influence.
The methodology description clearly lists volumes, replication numbers, and timings, enabling reproducibility with minimal ambiguities.
The independent variable section lists pH solutions well but lacks discussion of why chosen pH levels (2, 4, 7, 8, 10) are sufficient to explore the full response curve of mung bean growth.
The dependent variable description notes measurement to the nearest cm but should specify how measurement consistency is ensured (e.g., same observer, time of day) to reduce procedural variance.
The controlled variables list correctly identifies bean type and solution volume but does not detail how light intensity and humidity were monitored or held constant.
The ANOVA results are presented clearly but omit degrees of freedom and critical F-value; include these for a fully transparent statistical analysis.
The conclusion succinctly ties data trends and statistical tests to the research question, demonstrating internal consistency.
The scientific context comparison references one study but would benefit from integrating multiple sources or contrasting findings to deepen justification.
The strengths table effectively highlights even bean spacing but could link each strength back to how it minimizes experimental error specific to the research question.
The improvement suggested for roots growing into tissue paper is logical but would be clearer if rephrased as a concise action plan with specified materials.
The recommendation to prevent tissue paper drying by guaranteeing continuous water supply is practical; consider specifying exact volume and watering interval improvements.
The method list includes adequate replication (5 trials per pH) and clear steps for solution prep, demonstrating strong reproducibility.
The procedure omits precise timing/frequency of watering intervals over the 8 days; include a daily schedule to ensure consistent water delivery.
The qualitative results note mold influence but lack criteria for including or excluding affected beans in analysis; define treatment of anomalies clearly.
The raw data sample is helpful but only shows pH 2 and misaligns headers with data columns; include complete tables with labeled units for precision.
While the percentage variability formula is correct, the report does not reflect on why high values (>70 %) occur or their implication for data reliability.
The description of the Pearson r-value lacks detail on the calculation steps or formula; include the equation and a brief outline of computation for clarity.
In the processed data table, percentage variability at pH 10 exceeds 100 %, indicating either a computational or rounding error; review standard deviation inputs.
The main rootâgrowth chart is interpreted as showing an anomaly at pH 8, yet statistical justification is absent; consider adding confidence intervals or test statistics.
The interquartile range and outlier calculations are shown neatly but the report conflates âoutlierâ with âanomalyâ; clarify definitions and interpret accordingly.
The hypothesis testing section defines Hâ and Hâ but does not specify test assumptions (normality, homogeneity); include justification for ANOVA prerequisites.
The proposed extension using corn is relevant but would be strengthened by outlining specific adjustments to design (e.g., seed size differences, growth timeline).

The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.
